fuzzy_prd: We need a reusable polymorphic container library for C++ that allows any object meeting a declared set of behavioral contracts to be stored and operated on uniformly, without requiring a shared base class or inheritance hierarchy. The library must support four main scenarios: (1) A container that holds any of several unrelated shape-like objects and can invoke both a text-rendering operation and a numeric measurement — results use fixed-point notation with five decimal places, and unrecognized or mis-parameterized object kinds are rejected with a structured error signal; (2) A single named operation that carries multiple overloads dispatched by argument count, so callers with different argument shapes reach the correct implementation automatically; (3) An operation declared as optional, allowing the container to hold collections that may or may not implement it — when unavailable, a well-defined not-supported signal is raised instead of crashing; (4) Run-time type inspection and value recovery: the container reports a tag for what it holds, and a stored value can be retrieved by naming its expected type — a mismatch produces a neutral type-mismatch error. A thin adapter program reads a JSON command from standard input, routes it by action name to the appropriate handler, and writes results to standard output. The automated test runner script under rcb_tests saves raw per-case output for comparison. The core type-erasure machinery — concentrated in the main header file of the library — must remain completely decoupled from any I/O concerns.

trigger_keywords: what does the render operation print for rectangle
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A Rectangle with width W and height H is rendered as: {Rectangle: width = W, height = H}. A Circle with radius R is rendered as: {Circle: radius = R}. A Point is rendered as: {Point}. All numeric values (both inside the rendered text and in the area field) use fixed-point notation with exactly 5 fractional digits (std::fixed, std::setprecision(5)). The full output line format is: shape = <rendered text>, area = <measure value>\n

trigger_keywords: what happens with unknown shape kind
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When the command names an unrecognized kind (e.g., Triangle) or a recognized kind with the wrong number of parameters, the adapter emits exactly two lines: first 'error=invalid_command
', then 'command=<the raw command string>
'. For example, input command 'Triangle 2 3' yields: error=invalid_command
command=Triangle 2 3

trigger_keywords: info vs error log line format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A log entry with only a 'message' field is routed to the one-argument overload and rendered as '[INFO] <message>\n'. A log entry that also carries an 'error' field is routed to the two-argument overload and rendered as '[ERROR] <message> (exception info: <error>)\n'. Lines appear in the order supplied; the full accumulated buffer is emitted as a single output block.

trigger_keywords: what happens when lookup is called on sequential
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the optional keyed-lookup operation is invoked on a backing that does not support it (e.g., a sequential/list-backed collection), the library raises pro::not_implemented. The adapter catches this and emits exactly two lines: 'error=not_supported\n' then 'operation=lookup\n'. No crash or silent wrong result occurs. The indexed backing (std::vector) supports at() and returns the stored element; the sequential backing (std::list) does not implement at() so the fallback fires.

trigger_keywords: type inspect output for empty container
what does inspect_type return when stored is null
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the stored field is null (empty container), proxy_typeid(*p) returns typeid(void). The adapter checks t == typeid(void) and emits 'type=empty\n'. When a whole-number value is stored, proxy_typeid(*p) returns typeid(int), and the adapter emits 'type=integer\n'. The facade must include ::support<pro::skills::rtti> for proxy_typeid to be available.

trigger_keywords: what happens when expected type does not match stored type
hidden_constraints.oracle_response:
When proxy_cast<T>(*p) is called and T does not match the stored type (or the container is empty), pro::bad_proxy_cast is thrown. The adapter catches it and emits exactly two lines: 'error=type_mismatch
' then 'expected=<the expect field value>
'. When the types match, recovery succeeds and the adapter emits 'value=<recovered value>
'. The expect field accepts 'integer' (maps to int) or 'real' (maps to double).

trigger_keywords: how to declare an optional operation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An optional operation is declared using pro::weak_dispatch<DispatchTag> as the dispatch type in add_convention, e.g.: ::add_convention<pro::weak_dispatch<MemAt>, std::string(int)>. For MemAt backed by std::list, at() does not exist so the library raises pro::not_implemented at call time. The macro PRO_DEF_MEM_DISPATCH(MemAt, at, at) with two name arguments is used to define the dispatch for the optional operation in the dispatcher.

trigger_keywords: how are the three shape kinds provided to the container
allocate_proxy vs make_proxy vs pointer assignment
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The three shape kinds are provided through three different ownership strategies to demonstrate they all produce the same observable behavior: Rectangle is stored via pro::allocate_proxy<Drawable, Rectangle>(alloc, width, height) using a std::pmr::unsynchronized_pool_resource allocator; Circle is stored via pro::make_proxy<Drawable>(circle) from a copied value; Point is stored by assigning a raw pointer (&instance) to the proxy. All three strategies are accepted by the same pro::proxy<Drawable> container type.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: golden/proxy.h — the single-header implementation of the microsoft/proxy library (pro:: namespace), containing pro::proxy<F>, pro::facade_builder, pro::make_proxy, pro::allocate_proxy, pro::weak_dispatch, pro::skills::rtti, pro::not_implemented, pro::bad_proxy_cast, PRO_DEF_MEM_DISPATCH, etc.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the test runner script under rcb_tests
context_pointers.actual_meaning: rcb_tests/test.sh — the bash test harness that reads *.json case files from a cases directory (default: rcb_tests/public_test_cases/), runs each case through the dispatcher binary, and writes raw stdout to rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename.stem}@{case_index.zfill(3)}.txt

rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.cpp — a standalone C++ program that reads a single JSON object from stdin (using boost::json), routes on the 'action' field to one of five handlers (HandleRenderShape, HandleLog, HandleLookup, HandleInspectType, HandleRecoverValue), and writes results to stdout; it catches library-native exceptions and translates them to neutral error lines

pro::not_implemented — the exception type thrown by the proxy library when a weak_dispatch (optional) operation is invoked on a held type that does not implement the corresponding method; caught in HandleLookup and translated to 'error=not_supported\noperation=lookup\n'

pro::bad_proxy_cast — the exception type thrown by proxy_cast<T>(*p) when T does not match the actual stored type or the container is empty; caught in HandleRecoverValue and translated to 'error=type_mismatch\nexpected=<expect>\n'

oracle_data.fallback_response: The question does not match any specific documented constraint for the microsoft__proxy project. This project implements a type-erased polymorphic container (pro::proxy<F>) in C++ using the microsoft/proxy library. Core concepts include: declaring facades with pro::facade_builder and add_convention, dispatching operations with PRO_DEF_MEM_DISPATCH, optional operations with pro::weak_dispatch, run-time type inspection with pro::skills::rtti/proxy_typeid/proxy_cast, and three ownership strategies (make_proxy, allocate_proxy, raw pointer). The dispatcher adapter (rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatcher.cpp) routes five JSON actions: render_shape, log, lookup, inspect_type, recover_value. Please ask about a specific feature, output format, error condition, or API usage pattern.

feature1_polymorphic_dispatch.json.description: Build a single polymorphic container that can hold any object exposing a textual-render operation and a numeric-measure operation, then drive both operations through the container. A command string names which concrete kind of object to place inside (and supplies its parameters); unrelated kinds with no shared base type are all accepted by the same container. After the container is populated, its render operation writes a textual form of the held object and its measure operation returns a numeric value; the two are combined into one line. Numeric values are emitted in fixed-point notation with five fractional digits. A command that names an unknown kind, or supplies the wrong number of parameters, is rejected as an invalid command.

feature2_overloaded_operation.json.description: A single named operation on the container carries two overloads distinguished by how many arguments are supplied. A sequence of log entries is fed through the container; each entry with only a message is routed to the one-argument overload and rendered as an informational line, while an entry that also carries an error description is routed to the two-argument overload and rendered as an error line that embeds the error description. The held implementation accumulates every rendered line in order, and the full accumulated text is emitted.

feature3_optional_operation.json.description: The container declares a keyed-lookup operation as optional rather than mandatory, so it can hold backing collections that do not all provide that operation. When the held collection natively supports positional keyed lookup, looking up a key returns the stored element. When the held collection does not provide that operation, the lookup does not crash or silently misbehave; instead the framework reports a neutral not-supported signal naming the attempted operation. The chosen backing collection determines which path is taken.

feature4_1_stored_type.json.description: The container can report a tag describing the kind of value it currently holds. When the container is populated with a whole-number value, it reports an integer tag. When the container holds nothing at all, it reports an empty tag. The tag reflects only whether and what kind of value is stored, independent of the concrete value.

feature4_2_recover_value.json.description: A stored value can be recovered out of the container only by naming the type it was stored as. When the expected type matches the stored value's type, recovery succeeds and yields the original value. When the expected type does not match the stored value's type, recovery is rejected with a neutral type-mismatch error that echoes the expected type. Attempting to recover from an empty container is likewise rejected as a type mismatch.
